title: Accessibility
source: Starter KB based on WCAG 2.2 (W3C/WAI) and CVS Health Web Accessibility Annotation Kit guidance. Key references: https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/ ; https://www.w3.org/WAI/standards-guidelines/wcag/new-in-22/ ; https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/ ; https://github.com/cvs-health/annotations
purpose: Provide authoritative reference knowledge for evaluating and improving accessibility in UI/UX designs (web and mobile-inspired patterns) and for producing consistent accessibility annotations (e.g., greenlines, focus order, labels, landmarks) aligned with WCAG 2.2 and common enterprise review practices.
scope: Applies to user-facing UI design review: structure, navigation, focus, keyboard, touch targets, contrast, text alternatives, form labeling and errors, help and authentication usability, and assistive-technology compatibility. Includes practical mapping to an annotation workflow (e.g., CVS Web Accessibility Annotation Kit components). Excludes assistant behavior, tone, output formatting rules, schema enforcement, and runtime tool/DS rules.
definitions: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2: a W3C standard organized by four principles (Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust) with testable Success Criteria at A/AA/AAA levels.
The four WCAG principles: Perceivable (can be sensed), Operable (can be used), Understandable (can be understood), Robust (works with assistive tech).
A testable requirement in WCAG. Conformance is determined by meeting applicable SCs at a chosen level (A/AA/AAA).
A visible styling change that shows which element is currently focused for keyboard interaction (e.g., outline/ring).
The programmatic label announced by assistive technologies for a control (derived from label text, aria-label, etc.).

A common accessibility annotation technique that visually documents accessibility requirements on a design (e.g., labels, headings, focus order, landmarks, states) so engineers can implement them consistently.

A set of Figma components and patterns for documenting accessibility requirements (e.g., focus order, labels, landmarks) in design files; used as a reference mapping for how to annotate accessibility requirements.
rulesConstraints: Evaluate accessibility using POUR: ensure content is perceivable, operable via keyboard, understandable in language and interaction, and robust with assistive technologies.
Default target level for product UX validation is typically WCAG 2.2 AA unless stated otherwise.
Do not rely on color alone to convey meaning (errors, selection, status). Always provide text and/or iconography with accessible labeling.
Ensure interactive elements have a clear keyboard focus indicator and a logical focus order matching the visual and reading order.
Avoid focus being hidden by sticky headers, modals, or overlays; focused element must remain at least partially visible (WCAG 2.2 adds AA focus-not-obscured minimum).
Ensure touch targets are large enough and spaced to reduce accidental activation (WCAG 2.2 adds AA target size minimum).
Ensure drag-only interactions have a non-drag alternative (WCAG 2.2 adds AA dragging movements).
Forms must provide labels or instructions, identify errors in text, and provide suggestions for correction when possible.
Authentication flows must not depend solely on memory/complex puzzles; provide accessible alternatives and allow password managers (WCAG 2.2 adds accessible authentication minimum at AA).
When documenting accessibility in Figma, annotate requirements that are not reliably inferable from visuals alone (accessible name, role, state, focus order, error associations, keyboard behavior).

do: Use semantic structure: headings, sections, and groups should have meaningful hierarchy and labels.
Provide sufficient color contrast for text and essential UI elements; ensure non-text contrast for icons, focus rings, and controls.
Design for keyboard first: all controls operable without a mouse; tab order follows task flow; no keyboard traps.
Make focus visible and consistent across components; ensure focus is not clipped or obscured by overlays.
Design clear form labels (persistent labels preferred), required indicators, helper text, and field-level error messages bound to the field.
Provide error identification and recovery: state what’s wrong, where, and how to fix it; focus the first invalid field on submit where appropriate.
Ensure controls have clear accessible names that match visible labels; avoid mismatches between label text and the name announced to screen readers.
Design touch targets with minimum size and sufficient spacing; avoid tiny icon-only tap targets without padding.
Provide alternatives to gesture/drag-only interactions (buttons, stepper controls, plus/minus, menus).
Annotate: focus order, landmark/region intent, accessible names for ambiguous controls, error announcement requirements, and state behaviors (disabled, loading, expanded).
dont: Don’t use placeholder-only labels for inputs (placeholders disappear and reduce accessibility and usability).
Don’t rely on color alone to communicate errors or selection (e.g., red outline only).
Don’t hide focus indicators or make them too subtle to see against backgrounds.
Don’t place key actions behind hover-only UI on touch-first surfaces without an alternative.
Don’t require drag-only interactions without an equivalent control.
Don’t design authentication or security flows that block password managers or require memorization-only steps without alternatives.
Don’t ship forms that fail silently or show generic errors without field-level guidance.

examples: Annotate a numeric tab order on interactive elements: 1=Primary nav, 2=Search, 3=Primary CTA, etc. Note any skip link expectation for large navs.
Icon-only button shows a magnifying glass: annotate accessible name: “Search”. If there is visible text nearby, ensure the accessible name matches that text.
Field-level error: “Enter a valid email address (for example, name@example.com).” Annotate that the error message is programmatically associated with the input and announced on submit.
Annotate text contrast requirement for body text and link text, plus non-text contrast requirement for focus ring and input border states.
Annotate minimum tap target sizing for icon buttons and spacing between adjacent targets to prevent accidental activation.
If a card reorder uses drag handles, annotate: “Provide Move up/down buttons or menu actions as a non-drag alternative.”
edgeCases: When a design uses a custom component, explicitly annotate expected role, name, state, and keyboard interactions (e.g., for a custom dropdown: arrow keys, enter/escape behavior).
When content is highly dense (tables, dashboards), annotate reading order, keyboard navigation expectations, and any sticky headers that could obscure focus.
When using modals, annotate focus management: initial focus location, focus trap behavior, escape-to-close (if allowed), and focus return target when closed.
When errors occur in multi-step flows, annotate persistence of user input and whether progress is saved; ensure error summary links to fields for long forms.
When localization is expected, ensure labels and error messages remain concise and avoid relying on text length for layout; annotate truncation behavior.
When using CAPTCHAs, security challenges, or MFA, annotate accessible alternatives and compatibility with password managers and autofill.

If an org uses the CVS Web Accessibility Annotation Kit, map requirements to kit components consistently: focus order markers, label/name callouts, error association callouts, landmark/region callouts, and contrast/target-size notes.
